questions.text: I find that I am constantly scanning faces for emotional information.
I prefer relationships where the agenda emerges mutually.
I find that a single upsetting comment can color my entire day.
I find that over-preparation can make me rigid and slow to adapt when things change.
I do not naturally track the ebb and flow of conversational dynamics.
I would endure personal hardship rather than let someone I know struggle alone.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the influence that has done the most to shape the kind of friend, colleague, and community member I strive to be.
I think that emotional restraint during crises is a skill that produces measurably better outcomes for the people involved.
I frequently introduce new initiatives without waiting for a mandate.
I believe that strong leadership means facilitating agreement, not overriding it.
I hold the view that the people who do the most good in crises are those who can maintain analytical distance from the suffering around them.
My emotional reactions are gradual — they build slowly rather than arriving all at once.
I think of my daily habits as investments that pay dividends in the years ahead.
I find that I miss unspoken social expectations that others seem to just know.
I find that emotional upheaval in my environment doesn't destabilize me internally.
I don't care about streaks; what matters is whether I'm engaged and energized today.
I tend to define success in terms of my own benchmarks, not in terms of what I did for others.
I hold the view that personal growth should be a daily practice.
